skill.name: red-team-tools-and-methodology
skill.description: Provides authorized red-team and bug bounty methodology for reconnaissance, asset discovery, technology fingerprinting, and vulnerability triage.
skill.summary: Plan and run authorized web reconnaissance and vulnerability discovery workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static shell execution alerts are mostly Markdown code fences and documentation examples. They do not run automatically. The skill has medium dual-use risk because it teaches active recon, fuzzing, vulnerability scanning, API probing, and XSS testing.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Active Reconnaissance Workflows
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill provides commands for subdomain enumeration, host probing, fingerprinting, directory fuzzing, API enumeration, and vulnerability scanning. These workflows are legitimate for authorized testing. They can be misused against systems without permission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and surrounding text directly describe active reconnaissance and vulnerability scanning. Scope and bug bounty framing reduce concern about malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Automated XSS Testing Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes XSS hunting pipelines and payload reflection checks. This can help authorized research. It may also create unwanted traffic or exploit attempts outside scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly labels the workflow as automated XSS hunting and includes payload testing examples. The presence of scope and rate-limit constraints reduces concern about malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Runnable Reconnaissance Script Template
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The embedded shell script accepts a domain and runs subfinder, httpx, waybackurls, and nuclei. The script is not self-executing. Copying and running it can generate network scans and local output files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script is fully formed and operational when copied into a shell script. It still requires explicit user action and a target domain, which limits the severity.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution findings. The cited lines are Markdown fenced code blocks in SKILL.md. They do not execute automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown examples, confirmed by line-numbered review of SKILL.md. The commands inside the blocks are separately assessed as dual-use guidance.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to the skill description line, which contains red-team and bug bounty wording but no cryptographic function, algorithm, or implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is frontmatter description text only. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic algorithm is present at that location.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Safety Constraints
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The system reconnaissance alerts point to constraints telling users to avoid denial-of-service behavior and rate limit requests. These lines reduce operational risk rather than adding malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explicit safety guidance. They do not provide reconnaissance commands or hidden system collection behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Targets
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE with residual risk: Hardcoded URL alerts mostly reference placeholders such as target.com or a public BGP lookup page. They are not exfiltration endpoints. Users must replace placeholders only with authorized targets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples and placeholders, not hidden network destinations. They can still drive real traffic if copied without authorization.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Scanning and Fuzzing Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands use ffuf, httpx, httprobe, nuclei, amass, subfinder, and curl for discovery and vulnerability checks. Use requires explicit authorization and rate limits.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are direct network reconnaissance and scanning workflows. The file includes scope constraints, so the pattern is dangerous but not malicious by itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: XSS Payload Testing Pipeline
dangerous_patterns.description: The XSS sections include parameter collection, payload replacement, reflected response checks, and automated scanner use. These are sensitive testing workflows that need written permission.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes automated XSS hunting. The examples are clear enough to run against a target, but no stealth, persistence, or data theft is present.

content.value_statement: Security testers need repeatable workflows for finding assets, live hosts, endpoints, and likely vulnerabilities. This skill organizes authorized recon and bug bounty methods into practical steps.
actual_capabilities: Outlines a phased red-team workflow from scope setup to reporting.
Lists tools for subdomain enumeration, live host discovery, and technology fingerprinting.
Provides command examples for content discovery, API enumeration, and vulnerability scanning.
Describes application review priorities such as uploads, APIs, profiles, and integrations.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for rate limits, false positives, missing tools, and API keys.
limitations: It does not install or verify the listed security tools.
It does not confirm permission or legal scope for any target.
It may produce false positives that need manual validation.
It is focused on web and bug bounty workflows, not full enterprise operations.

use_cases.title: Prepare Bug Bounty Recon
use_cases.description: Build a scoped plan for discovering domains, live hosts, URLs, technologies, and high-value test areas before manual review.

use_cases.title: Standardize Web App Assessment
use_cases.description: Use a repeatable checklist for asset discovery, API review, content discovery, scanner output, and finding validation.

use_cases.title: Train Security Analysts
use_cases.description: Teach analysts how common recon tools fit together and where authorization, rate limits, and manual verification matter.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Safe Recon Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for an authorized target.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a red-team recon plan for my authorized target. Include phases, required inputs, rate-limit reminders, and expected deliverables.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Recon Tools
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate tool selection for a web program.
prompt_templates.prompt: Map the tools in this skill to my bug bounty scope. Explain what each tool collects, what permissions it needs, and which outputs I should save.

prompt_templates.title: Review Scanner Results
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced triage after running approved tests.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me triage authorized recon and scanner results. Separate likely false positives from findings that need manual proof and reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Controlled Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a controlled recon workflow for a large authorized scope. Include concurrency limits, logging, validation steps, and stop conditions.

output_examples.input: Plan recon for an authorized target with one root domain.
output_examples.output: A phased checklist covering scope confirmation, subdomain discovery, live host checks, technology review, content discovery, scanning, and reporting notes.

output_examples.input: Explain how to prioritize a web application review.
output_examples.output: A priority list focused on file uploads, APIs, profile fields, integrations, error pages, authentication boundaries, and past security history.

output_examples.input: Help validate noisy vulnerability scanner results.
output_examples.output: A triage workflow that groups findings by severity, removes duplicates, confirms evidence manually, records notes, and flags uncertain results.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, scope, and rate limits before running any command.
Store raw outputs and manual validation notes so findings can be reproduced.
Treat scanner results as leads and verify impact before reporting.
anti_patterns: Do not run fuzzing, scanning, or payload tests against systems outside scope.
Do not submit automated scanner output without manual validation and impact analysis.
Do not ignore program rules, production safety limits, or denial-of-service restrictions.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for public marketplace use?
faq.answer: It is medium risk because it contains dual-use security testing workflows. It should be used only for authorized targets.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The commands are Markdown examples and require a user to copy and run them.

faq.question: What tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It references tools such as Amass, Subfinder, httpx, httprobe, ffuf, Nuclei, Burp Suite, Dalfox, and waybackurls.

faq.question: Can it replace a penetration testing methodology?
faq.answer: No. It is a practical checklist and command guide, not a complete legal, reporting, or quality program.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: Some workflows may use external services such as Shodan or Censys, but the skill does not collect or transmit keys itself.

faq.question: What should users do before scanning?
faq.answer: Users should confirm scope, obtain permission, set rate limits, and understand the target program rules.
